title: Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships
summary: Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships retrieves prior HTML skills in an original semantic readiness exam scenario and requires source, parsed-structure, keyboard, and content evidence.
objectives: Apply semantic landmarks, semantic heading outline, semantic text meaning, semantic time address quotes, semantic disclosure, semantic content models, semantic native first in unfamiliar content.
Produce or audit a real semantic readiness exam artifact without appearance-only reasoning.
Keep earlier document requirements active while verifying native behavior and valid relationships.

steps.title: predict 1: regional heat-response portal audit · quiz
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim preserves meaning in the changed case? Scenario: semantic readiness exam.
steps.why: Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships keeps meaning testable instead of depending on appearance. This is checkpoint 1 in semantic readiness exam.

content.title: semantic readiness exam evidence
content.text: Choose the claim that preserves content meaning when visual styling and browser recovery are removed.

hints: Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 1: name the content relationship before choosing markup.
Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 1: compare source, parsed structure, and keyboard route.
Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 1: choose the smallest native relationship that survives changed styling.

options.text: Semantic readiness exam case 1: header, section, article, aside, and div are interchangeable boxes once CSS makes them look identical.
Semantic readiness exam case 1: choose each region from its content relationship and navigation purpose, then verify the resulting landmark map.
Semantic readiness exam case 1: use main for every visually large panel so assistive technology finds all important boxes.

options.text: semantic readiness exam phase 2.1: name the complete page topic
semantic readiness exam phase 2.2: identify its major content sections
semantic readiness exam phase 2.3: nest subsections beneath their actual parents
semantic readiness exam phase 2.4: read the heading-only outline and correct broken relationships

hints: Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 3: name the content relationship before choosing markup.

Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 3: choose the smallest native relationship that survives changed styling.

options.text: Semantic readiness exam case 3: strong and em are merely longer spellings of bold and italic, so they can style any text without changing meaning.
Semantic readiness exam case 3: select inline elements from the meaning the sentence needs, and leave purely visual treatment to CSS.
Semantic readiness exam case 3: use strong for every heading because bold text is always more semantic.

hints: Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 4: name the content relationship before choosing markup.
Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 4: compare source, parsed structure, and keyboard route.
Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 4: choose the smallest native relationship that survives changed styling.

options.text: Semantic readiness exam case 4: address is the correct wrapper for any street location, even when it is not contact information for the page or article.
Semantic readiness exam case 4: use time, address, quotation, and citation elements only when their defined relationship matches the content.
Semantic readiness exam case 4: use cite as a universal wrapper around the name of any quoted speaker.

Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 5: choose the smallest native relationship that survives changed styling.

options.text: Semantic readiness exam case 5: any control that opens hidden content should be implemented with details, including navigation and modal dialogs.
Semantic readiness exam case 5: choose details and summary only when a native disclosure matches the information task and expected focus behavior.
Semantic readiness exam case 5: build every disclosure from a clickable div so the native marker never appears.

steps.title: predict 6: invalid parent-child incident · quiz

content.title: semantic readiness exam evidence

hints: Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 6: name the content relationship before choosing markup.
Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 6: compare source, parsed structure, and keyboard route.
Readiness Exam: Semantic Relationships checkpoint 6: choose the smallest native relationship that survives changed styling.

options.text: Semantic readiness exam case 6: if invalid nesting produces the intended pixels, the parent-child relationship has no user or interoperability cost.
Semantic readiness exam case 6: check the element content model, validate the source, and compare the parsed DOM before accepting the nesting.
Semantic readiness exam case 6: add role presentation to invalid children so the parser keeps them in place.

options.text: semantic readiness exam phase 7.1: classify the task as navigation, action, input, progress, or disclosure
semantic readiness exam phase 7.2: select the native element with the matching behavior contract
semantic readiness exam phase 7.3: add only attributes and styling required by the design
semantic readiness exam phase 7.4: verify names, states, focus, keyboard behavior, and fallback

options.text: Semantic readiness exam case 13: if invalid nesting produces the intended pixels, the parent-child relationship has no user or interoperability cost.
Semantic readiness exam case 13: check the element content model, validate the source, and compare the parsed DOM before accepting the nesting.
Semantic readiness exam case 13: indentation overrides the content model because it makes author intent obvious to the browser.

options.text: Semantic readiness exam case 14: a styled div with role button is more professional than button because native controls are too limited.
Semantic readiness exam case 14: start with the native element whose complete behavior matches the task, then style it without deleting its contract.
Semantic readiness exam case 14: replace native controls with generic elements first and add keyboard behavior only if a test fails.
